title: Painting over mould and damp stains
subtitle: Treat the source first, then seal and redecorate
excerpt: The correct sequence for dealing with mould and damp staining on interior walls: identifying the moisture source, treating the existing mould, applying a stain-block primer, and repainting so the stain does not bleed through.
sourceNotes: NHS guidance on damp and mould in homes (publicly available). Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Board of Agrément technical guidance on damp treatments.

content.text: Painting over mould without treating it first traps the mould beneath the paint. It regrows, typically within a season. Painting over a damp stain without a
stain-block primer

leaves a brown ring that bleeds through every subsequent topcoat. The correct order is: identify and fix the moisture source, treat the mould, apply the stain block, redecorate. Skip any of these steps and the problem returns.
Identifying the source

Mould on an external wall in a cold area (corner of a room, below a window) is almost always condensation. The wall is too cold; warm moist air from the room condenses on it. Improving insulation and ventilation is the fix. Mould on an internal wall or ceiling, or a brown tide-mark stain, usually means a water ingress (a leaking pipe, a failed roof or gutter, a cracked pointing). Fix the ingress before treating the wall.
Treating the mould
Step 1, apply a fungicidal wash.

A proprietary fungicidal wash (sodium hypochlorite solution, commonly sold as mould remover) applied to the affected area kills the active mould spores. Apply with a cloth or sponge, allow the contact time stated on the product, then wipe off. Wear protective gloves; hypochlorite bleaches fabric and skin.
Step 2, prepare the surface.

Once the fungicidal wash has dried (minimum 24 hours), sand the surface lightly to knock back any raised paint edges around the stain. Scrape off any loose or flaking paint. Vacuum and wipe the surface clean.
Sealing the stain
Step 3, apply an oil-based stain block.

Apply the stain-block primer with a brush, covering the stained area and extending 50 mm beyond its visible edge. The stain-block will smell strongly of solvent; ventilate the room. Allow to dry fully per the product data sheet (typically 30 to 60 minutes for shellac-based products). A second coat may be needed on severe brown water stains.

Water-based stain blocks smell less, but oil-based shellac products work better on heavy damp stains and nicotine staining. For light mould marks with no visible watermark, a water-based product is fine.
Redecoration
Step 4, apply a fungicidal emulsion topcoat.

For condensation-prone walls, use an emulsion with an integral fungicide. Apply a mist coat over the stain-blocked area before the full-strength topcoat. Most standard emulsions will cover the primed surface correctly; the issue is at the boundary between the primed and unprimed area, where the sheen may differ. Extending the topcoat at least 100 mm beyond the stain-blocked area blends the finish.
Long-term prevention

Condensation mould comes back unless the wall surface gets warmer (better insulation), the room gets drier (better ventilation), or both. An extractor fan running for 20 minutes after bathing or cooking helps. A trickle vent in the window frame helps. Thermal curtain lining helps. These are the three cheapest fixes.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Re-investigate the moisture source. If the source is fixed, apply an oil-based stain-block primer and repaint.
attrs.cause: The moisture source is still active, or the stain-block primer was not applied, or a water-based stain block was used on a severe stain.
attrs.symptom: Brown ring reappears through new paint within weeks

attrs.fix: Treat the mould again. Address the source: improve ventilation, reduce humidity, or add a layer of thermal insulation to the affected wall to raise the surface temperature above the dew point.
attrs.cause: The ventilation problem was not addressed.
attrs.symptom: Mould returns in the same patch within months
